5 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I started learning Flutter, I did what everyone does.
I searched YouTube.
I opened the official documentation.
I watched tutorial after tutorial.
After a while, I noticed something.
Almost every tutorial assumes you’re completely new to programming.
There’s nothing wrong with that. But after spending several years writing JavaScript, I didn’t need someone to explain what a package manager was.
What I really wanted was this:
Explain Flutter as if I already know frontend
I couldn’t find that series, so I decided to write it myself.
This series is basically my notes after reading the official Dart and Flutter documentation, watching lots of tutorials, and making plenty of mistakes along the way.
If you’re a React, Vue, or JavaScript developer, I hope this saves you some time.
Flutter is written by Dart, a language kinda like Java, I think so. When I run the project, Dart will goes from top to bottom of every file and then compile it into native code (depend on what’s you build). Like If you run on ios device, it will compile to swift, etc…
Let’s begin with the first chapter.
Installation
Before installing Flutter, there’s one thing I wish someone had told me.
The official documentation recommends downloading the Flutter SDK directly onto your computer, and there’s absolutely nothing wrong with that.
https://docs.flutter.dev/get-started/install
However, it reminded me of installing Node.js manually. It works perfectly fine until one day you need another version for a different project.
That’s why I chose FVM (Flutter Version Manager) instead.
If you’ve used JavaScript before, just think of it as NVM for Flutter. It lets you switch Flutter versions for different projects without reinstalling everything. From what I’ve seen, this is also how most mobile developers manage their Flutter SDK.
You can install it here:
https://fvm.app
Once everything is installed, run:
fvm flutter doctorIf everything looks good, you’re ready to go.
One small tip that would’ve saved me some time: if you’re only planning to build an iOS application, you don’t need Android Studio. VS Code with the Flutter and Dart extensions is enough for getting started. Those extensions provide syntax highlighting, code completion, debugging support, and a few handy Flutter commands.
Creating Your First Project
Creating a Flutter project is surprisingly simple.
Open VS Code, press Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + P, and search for Flutter.
Choose Flutter: New Project, select Application, then pick a folder where you’d like to create it.
A few seconds later, Flutter generates the project for you.
Then I opened the project and immediately had my first question.
“Why are there so many folders?”
Understanding the Folder Structure
At first glance, a Flutter project looks much bigger than a typical React project, here’s what I learned after spending some time with Flutter: you only need to understand a few things in the beginning.
The most important folder is lib/.
This is where you’ll write almost all of your application code. If you’re coming from React, you can think of it as your src/ folder. Inside it, you’ll find main.dart, which is the application’s entry point, similar to main.tsx in a React application.
The next important file is pubspec.yaml.
If you’ve worked with JavaScript before, it’ll immediately remind you of package.json. It contains your project’s dependencies, metadata, assets, and fonts. One difference is that Flutter requires you to explicitly declare your assets and fonts before using them.
You’ll also notice a file named pubspec.lock. You can think of it as Flutter’s version of package-lock.json. Most of the time, you won’t edit it manually.
The remaining files aren’t very important on your first day, but it’s useful to know what they do.

• build/: Similar to dist/ or build/ in frontend projects. Flutter stores generated and compiled files here. If you delete it, Flutter will recreate it.

• .dart_tool/: Stores build caches and package resolution data. It’s closer to node_modules/.cache than node_modules itself.

• analysis_options.yaml: Contains linting and analyzer rules, similar to an ESLint configuration.

• .metadata: Stores information about the Flutter project, such as the Flutter version and migration state.
Finally, you’ll see several platform folders like android/, ios/, web/, macos/, windows/, and linux/.
You write your application once inside the lib/ folder. When you build the project, Flutter compiles your Dart code into native machine code and packages it inside the corresponding platform project.
For example, building for Android produces an APK or AAB using the android/ project. Building for iOS packages the exact same application through the ios/ project. The same idea applies to desktop and web.
The important thing to remember is that you aren’t writing six different applications. You’re writing one application, and Flutter takes care of packaging it for each platform.
Wrapping Up
Throughout this series, I’ll keep explaining Flutter from the perspective of a frontend developer. Whenever possible, we’ll start with something familiar from JavaScript, then see how Flutter solves the same problem. I’ll also share the mistakes I made and the little things I wish someone had told me earlier.
If you think I’m moving too fast or spending too much time on a topic, let me know. I’m still learning too, and I’d love to make this series useful for anyone making the same journey.
See you in the next chapter!!!
